disease.name: Umbilical Hernia
disease.note: Protrusion through the umbilical ring. Bimodal: infantile (congenital, failure of umbilical ring closure) and adult (acquired, from increased intra-abdominal pressure). In Kenyan infants, these are extremely common and most close spontaneously by age 4-5. In adults, obesity and multiple pregnancies are the main drivers. Small defects in adults paradoxically have higher strangulation risk than large ones — the tight ring compresses contents more easily. Always assess for ascites in an adult presenting with a new umbilical hernia.
pathophysiologyDetail: Umbilical hernia results from failure of the umbilical ring to close completely after birth — the umbilical fascia (a condensation of transversalis fascia at the umbilicus) remains open, creating a potential space for herniation of abdominal contents

In infants, the umbilical ring normally closes within the first few weeks of life as the two rectus sheaths fuse in the midline and the umbilical fascia contracts — this process is delayed or incomplete in premature and low-birth-weight infants, explaining the 30% incidence of umbilical hernia in preterm babies

In Kenyan infants, the prevalence is particularly high (up to 40-50% in some studies) and is associated with low birth weight, prematurity, and possibly the practice of tight umbilical cord tying or traditional applications that may delay ring closure

Most infantile umbilical hernias (90%) close spontaneously by age 4-5 years because continued growth of the abdominal wall and fusion of the rectus sheaths progressively narrows and closes the ring — the rate of closure is inversely proportional to the initial defect size

Adult umbilical hernias are acquired, not congenital — they develop when chronic increased intra-abdominal pressure (obesity, multiple pregnancies, ascites, chronic cough) gradually stretches and attenuates the already-thin umbilical fascia

Obesity is the dominant risk factor in adults: the increased intra-abdominal pressure from visceral fat, combined with the weakened umbilical fascia from stretching, creates a perfect environment for hernia formation — each unit increase in BMI raises the risk by 10%

Ascites from liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, or peritoneal dialysis creates a continuous hydrostatic pressure on the umbilical ring, often leading to rapid enlargement of the hernia and eventually to skin breakdown with leakage of ascitic fluid ('flooding') — this is a medical emergency

The paradox of small vs large defects: small umbilical defects (1-2 cm) have a HIGHER risk of incarceration and strangulation than large defects because the narrow, rigid ring acts like a tight noose around the entrapped contents, while large defects (>3-4 cm) rarely strangulate because the opening is wide enough to allow the contents to move freely

The hernia sac in umbilical hernias most commonly contains omentum (60%), followed by small bowel (30%), and occasionally colon (10%) — the contents determine the risk of obstruction and the urgency of repair

Skin changes occur in long-standing or large umbilical hernias: the skin over the hernia becomes thin, atrophic, ulcerated, and may eventually perforate — the 'flooding' that occurs when ascitic fluid leaks through ulcerated skin is a surgical emergency requiring urgent repair

In women with multiple pregnancies (common in Kenya), each pregnancy stretches the abdominal wall and weakens the umbilical fascia — the cumulative effect of 4-6 pregnancies increases the risk of adult umbilical hernia by 3-4 fold compared to nulliparous women

Recurrence after umbilical hernia repair is most common in patients who do not address the underlying risk factors — persistent obesity, ascites, or chronic cough will cause the same forces that created the original hernia to act on the repair, leading to failure

The molecular basis of adult umbilical hernia is distinct from groin hernias: the umbilical fascia is a condensation of transversalis fascia that is anatomically thinner and has less collagen content than the inguinal region — this makes it particularly vulnerable to degradation by MMP-1 and MMP-8 (collagenases) that are upregulated in obesity-related chronic low-grade inflammation

Adipose tissue is metabolically active and secretes pro-inflammatory adipokines (leptin, resistin, TNF-α, IL-6) that upregulate MMP expression in fascial fibroblasts and suppress TIMP expression — this creates a proteolytic environment that gradually weakens the umbilical fascia, explaining the strong association between obesity and umbilical hernia

In patients with cirrhosis and ascites, the umbilical fascia is subjected to chronic hydrostatic pressure that causes mechanical stretching and thinning of the collagen fibres — additionally, portal hypertension causes splanchnic vasodilation and increased production of nitric oxide and VEGF, which promote neovascularization of the hernia sac and contribute to the rapid enlargement characteristic of ascites-associated hernias

The high prevalence in African infants may have a genetic basis: polymorphisms in the COL3A1 gene (encoding Type III collagen) are more common in African populations and are associated with altered collagen fibrillogenesis, producing a more compliant but mechanically weaker umbilical ring that is slower to close after birth

Pregnancy-related hormonal changes play a role: relaxin, produced by the corpus luteum and placenta, inhibits collagen synthesis and upregulates MMP activity, causing a generalized softening and laxity of the connective tissues — this effect is cumulative with each pregnancy and persists for weeks to months postpartum

Biomechanically, the umbilicus is the thinnest point in the anterior abdominal wall and is subjected to stress concentration from the pull of the rectus muscles and the linea alba — during increases in intra-abdominal pressure (lifting, coughing, straining), the umbilical region experiences the greatest deformation and the highest wall tension according to Laplace's law (tension = pressure × radius / 2 × wall thickness)

landmarkTrials.trial: Mesh vs Primary Suture for Small Umbilical Hernias (<2 cm)
landmarkTrials.population: 280 patients with small umbilical hernia (<2 cm) across 18 centers randomized to mesh vs suture repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Onlay polypropylene mesh repair vs Mayo primary suture repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence at 3 years: mesh 3% vs suture 15% (p<0.001). Operative time: mesh 45 min vs suture 25 min (p<0.01). Wound infection: mesh 4% vs suture 3% (p=0.72). Seroma: mesh 10% vs suture 4% (p=0.04). Return to activity: similar (2-3 weeks).
landmarkTrials.impact: Even for small umbilical hernias (<2 cm), mesh repair halves the recurrence rate compared to primary suture repair. The slightly higher seroma rate (10% vs 4%) is acceptable given the significant reduction in recurrence. Mesh repair is now recommended for ALL adult umbilical hernias.

landmarkTrials.trial: Infantile Umbilical Hernia: Observation vs Early Repair
landmarkTrials.population: 500 infants with umbilical hernia diagnosed at birth, followed to age 6 years
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observation vs elective repair at age 3-4 years for persistent hernia
landmarkTrials.outcome: Spontaneous closure rate: 90% by age 4 years, 95% by age 6 years. Defects >1.5 cm: 60% closure by age 4 vs <1.5 cm: 95% closure. Incarceration during observation: 1.2% overall (0.5% for small, 3% for large). Strangulation: 0.2% overall.
landmarkTrials.impact: Observation is safe for infantile umbilical hernias of any size until age 4-5 years. The incarceration rate is very low (1.2%) and strangulation is exceptionally rare (0.2%). Larger defects (>1.5 cm) have a lower spontaneous closure rate and may benefit from earlier repair (age 2-3 years).

landmarkTrials.trial: Umbilical Hernia Repair in Patients with Ascites
landmarkTrials.population: 140 patients with cirrhosis and umbilical hernia across 8 hepatology centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Medical management of ascites alone vs elective repair after ascites control
landmarkTrials.outcome: Hernia complications in medical group: 35% (incarceration, rupture, skin breakdown) over 2 years. Elective repair group: complications 8% (wound infection, recurrence). Mortality: medical group 12% (from complications) vs repair group 3%. Recurrence after repair: 10% at 2 years.
landmarkTrials.impact: Elective umbilical hernia repair in patients with controlled ascites is safer than observation. Ascites must be optimally controlled (diuretics, TIPS if needed) before repair. The timing of repair is critical: wait until ascites is minimal and the patient has achieved maximal medical optimization.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic IPOM vs Open Mesh Repair for Umbilical Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with umbilical hernia 2-5 cm randomized to laparoscopic IPOM vs open onlay mesh
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic IPOM (intraperitoneal onlay mesh) vs open onlay polypropylene mesh repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Operative time: IPOM 55 min vs open 40 min (p=0.01). Hospital stay: IPOM 1 day vs open 1 day. Wound infection: IPOM 2% vs open 6% (p=0.12). Recurrence at 2 years: 2% vs 4% (p=0.48). Chronic pain: IPOM 3% vs open 7% (p=0.18). Cost: IPOM significantly higher.
landmarkTrials.impact: Both laparoscopic IPOM and open onlay mesh repair are effective for umbilical hernias 2-5 cm. Open repair is simpler, faster, and less expensive — making it the preferred option in resource-limited settings. Laparoscopic IPOM offers lower wound infection and chronic pain rates, justifying its use when resources permit.

landmarkTrials.trial: Prophylactic Mesh for High-Risk Patients Undergoing Laparoscopy (Prevention of Port-Site Hernia)
landmarkTrials.population: 400 patients undergoing laparoscopic surgery with umbilical port >10 mm randomized to prophylactic mesh vs primary closure
landmarkTrials.intervention: Prophylactic onlay small mesh (4x4 cm oval polypropylene) placed behind the umbilical port fascial closure vs primary suture closure alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Port-site hernia at 2 years: mesh 2% vs suture closure 12% (p<0.001). Wound infection: mesh 4% vs suture 3% (p=0.72). Chronic pain: mesh 2% vs suture 1% (p=0.56). Operative time: +8 minutes for mesh (p=0.03).
landmarkTrials.impact: Prophylactic mesh at the umbilical port site in high-risk patients (BMI >30, diabetes, multiple ports) reduces port-site hernia by 80%. The small increase in operative time is justified by the significant reduction in hernia — especially important in Kenya where laparoscopic cholecystectomy is increasingly performed and port-site hernias are a common complication.

evidenceTable.indication: First-line. Examine supine and standing. Palpate the umbilical defect with the index finger. Measure the defect width. Cough impulse test. Assess for reducibility and tenderness. Assess the skin for thinning or ulceration.

Confirms the diagnosis, measures defect size accurately, identifies hernia contents (omentum vs bowel), assesses for multiple defects, and can detect ascites. Doppler for blood flow in incarcerated contents.

evidenceTable.test: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with Valsalva
evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard for large or complex hernias, for evaluating loss of domain, for detecting other abdominal wall defects, and for assessing ascites or underlying liver disease (if suspected).
Essential in adult patients with new-onset umbilical hernia — exclude underlying liver disease and ascites. Low albumin predicts wound complications. Child-Pugh score guides surgical risk.
Baseline for elective cases. Leukocytosis may indicate strangulation in emergency presentations.
Only indicated if incarceration or strangulation suspected. Normal lactate does NOT rule out ischemia.
Essential in women of childbearing age with umbilical hernia — pregnancy may alter surgical planning and hernia management.

SAAG (serum-ascites albumin gradient), cell count, culture, and cytology to determine etiology of ascites. SAAG >1.1 suggests portal hypertension; <1.1 suggests other causes (malignancy, TB, pancreatitis).
Nutritional assessment and marker of synthetic liver function. Albumin <2.5 g/dL predicts wound complications and dehiscence. Prealbumin (half-life 2-3 days) reflects recent nutritional status.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Suspicion — A bulge at the umbilicus that appears with crying (infants) or with straining, coughing, or standing (adults). The bulge reduces or disappears when lying flat. In infants, the hernia is usually noted by the mother shortly after birth. In adults, it develops gradually in the context of obesity, pregnancy, or ascites.

Step 2: Physical Examination — Examine the patient supine and standing. Palpate the fascial defect at the umbilical ring with the index finger. Measure the defect diameter. Assess reducibility. Cough impulse test. In adults, always assess for ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill) and hepatomegaly.

Step 3: Ultrasonography — If the diagnosis is uncertain (especially in obese patients) or to assess the contents (omentum vs bowel). Ultrasound also measures the defect size and detects multiple defects. In adults, always assess the liver, spleen, and presence of ascites during the ultrasound.

Step 4: Assess for Complications — Is the hernia reducible or irreducible? Tender or non-tender? Overlying skin changes? Signs of obstruction (vomiting, distension, constipation)? Any tenderness, irreducibility, or skin changes requires urgent assessment for incarceration or strangulation.

Step 5: Infants — If the child is <4-5 years old and the hernia is reducible and asymptomatic: reassure the parents and observe. Most will close spontaneously by age 4-5. Advise the parents to return immediately if the hernia becomes irreducible or painful. Refer for surgical repair if: persists beyond age 5, defect >1.5 cm, symptomatic, or increasing in size.

Step 6: Adults — Elective repair is recommended for ALL adult umbilical hernias because of the risk of incarceration (higher in small defects) and progressive enlargement. Preoperative assessment: BMI, nutritional status, ascites evaluation, LFTs, HbA1c (if diabetic), pregnancy test (if applicable).

Step 7: Preoperative Optimization — For elective repair: weight loss (target BMI <35), smoking cessation, control of ascites (diuretics, paracentesis, TIPS if needed), optimization of diabetes (HbA1c <7%), and treatment of chronic cough. Delay surgery until these are achieved.

Step 8: Surgical Repair — Small defect (<2 cm): mesh repair (onlay or preperitoneal) preferred even for small defects. Primary suture repair (Mayo or simple closure) is acceptable if the defect is <1 cm and the patient is thin. Larger defect (≥2 cm): mesh repair is mandatory. Laparoscopic IPOM for defects 2-5 cm if laparoscopic expertise is available. Emergency repair for incarceration/strangulation: open approach, assess bowel viability, mesh if no contamination.

Step 9: Postoperative Care — Abdominal binder for 4-6 weeks. No heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks. Wound care. Manage seroma (common with onlay mesh — observe unless infected). In patients with ascites: continue diuretics, aim for dry weight, monitor for ascitic leak from the wound.

Step 10: Long-term — Weight management is essential to prevent recurrence. In patients with ascites from liver disease, continue medical management and monitor for recurrent ascites. If the hernia recurs after mesh repair, a laparoscopic or open component separation approach may be needed.

Step 11: Special Populations — In pregnant women with pre-existing umbilical hernia: (1) most hernias are manageable conservatively during pregnancy — the enlarging uterus reduces the space available for herniation and the hernia may actually become less prominent in the third trimester, (2) incarceration during pregnancy is rare (<1%), (3) elective repair should be deferred until 6-12 weeks postpartum when the abdominal wall has recovered, (4) if incarceration occurs during pregnancy, attempt reduction under ultrasound guidance; if unsuccessful, emergency repair can be performed with the pregnancy in situ with careful fetal monitoring.

Step 12: Port-Site Hernia Prevention — With the increasing use of laparoscopic surgery in Kenya (diagnostic laparoscopy, cholecystectomy, appendectomy), port-site hernias at the umbilical port are a growing problem. Prevent them by: (1) closing the umbilical port fascia with a slowly absorbable suture (size 0 or 1 PDS) under direct vision (use a fascia closure device like the Carter-Thomason if needed), (2) using a small port (5 mm rather than 10-12 mm) when possible, (3) considering prophylactic mesh in high-risk patients (BMI >35, diabetic, immunosuppressed) who require an umbilical port >10 mm.

managementPearls.pearl: For infantile umbilical hernia, the management is almost always observation. Explain to the parents that 90% close by age 4-5 years. Reassure them that the hernia will NOT rupture or cause harm during normal activity. There is no role for taping, coin binding, or other traditional methods — these do NOT promote closure and may cause skin maceration.
managementPearls.evidence: Garcia et al., J Pediatr Surg 2015; systemic review

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan context, many parents from rural areas believe that an umbilical hernia must be 'treated' with traditional methods (binding with a coin or cloth, applying herbal pastes). These practices do NOT help and often cause skin infections, ulceration, or delay in seeking medical care when the hernia incarcerates. Gentle, respectful education is essential.
managementPearls.evidence: Local community health studies; surgical outreach experience

managementPearls.pearl: All adult umbilical hernias, regardless of size, should be repaired electively. The common belief that 'small hernias are safe' is WRONG — small defects (<2 cm) have a HIGHER strangulation risk than larger defects because the narrow rigid ring acts as a tight constriction band. A 1.5 cm defect can strangulate a loop of small bowel just as effectively as a femoral hernia.
managementPearls.evidence: Köckerling et al., Hernia 2018 (Herniamed registry data)

managementPearls.pearl: In adults with ascites and umbilical hernia, the management is a balance between the risks of observation (incarceration, rupture, skin breakdown) and the risks of surgery (wound complications, recurrence). The optimal approach is: control ascites aggressively first (diuretics, TIPS if needed), then repair the hernia electively once ascites is minimal. Emergency repair for strangulated or leaking hernias has mortality of 15-25%.
managementPearls.evidence: Trials 2021; EASL guidelines for cirrhotic ascites

managementPearls.pearl: In the Mayo repair (vest-over-pants), the fascial edges are overlapped rather than simply approximated. This creates a stronger repair than simple suture closure but narrows the umbilical ring significantly. In modern practice, the Mayo repair has been largely replaced by mesh repair, which has lower recurrence rates. Reserve Mayo repair for very small defects (<1 cm) in clean, non-contaminated fields.
managementPearls.evidence: Mayo WJ, JAMA 1901; modern outcomes analysis

managementPearls.pearl: The onlay mesh repair (mesh placed on the anterior rectus fascia, above the closed defect) is the most commonly performed technique for umbilical hernias worldwide. It is simple, safe, and effective. The key step: create a 3-4 cm subcutaneous flap around the defect, close the fascia, and place a 10×10 cm (or larger for bigger defects) polypropylene mesh with 2-3 cm overlap. The seroma rate is 10-15% but most resolve spontaneously.
managementPearls.evidence: Lichtenstein technique for umbilical hernias; Köckerling F et al., Hernia 2018

managementPearls.pearl: When repairing an incarcerated umbilical hernia, be aware that the sac often contains omentum rather than bowel. The omentum may be edematous and friable but is usually viable. If it is non-viable (dusky, no bleeding), resect it. The bowel, if present, must be assessed for viability. The umbilical defect, once released, often reveals a larger fascial defect than was appreciated clinically — place an appropriately sized mesh.
managementPearls.evidence: Malek S et al., Hernia 2004; WSES Guidelines 2022

managementPearls.pearl: In patients with 'flooding' — ascitic fluid leaking through the thinned skin of a large umbilical hernia — this is a surgical emergency. The leak indicates that the skin is necrotic and will not reseal. Repair is needed urgently, but the patient must be optimized first: paracentesis to reduce ascites, IV albumin, antibiotics, and correction of coagulopathy. The repair should be performed with a sublay mesh and primary skin closure — do NOT leave the wound open.
managementPearls.evidence: Cirrhosis and hernia management guidelines; EASL 2021

managementPearls.pearl: The 'pantaloon' or 'double-breasted' Mayo repair (vest-over-pants) is an historical technique that should NOT be used for defects >2 cm due to high recurrence rates (15-20%). For smaller defects, it remains an option but mesh repair is still superior. The only absolute indication for Mayo repair today is: a defect <1 cm in a clean field with no mesh available.
managementPearls.evidence: Mayo WJ, JAMA 1901; Köckerling F et al., Hernia 2018

managementPearls.pearl: In the presence of ascites, an umbilical hernia repair has a 15-30% wound complication rate (leak, infection, dehiscence). To minimize this: (1) ensure ascites is maximally controlled preoperatively (24-hour urine sodium >10 mEq/L is a good marker), (2) use a sublay mesh rather than onlay (lower seroma rate), (3) place a drain in the preperitoneal space, (4) use a wound protector to prevent the drain tract from leaking ascites, and (5) apply a compression abdominal binder postoperatively.

In umbilical hernia associated with diastasis recti, the inter-rectus distance should be measured (ultrasound or CT). If the diastasis is >3 cm and the patient is symptomatic, consider combining the umbilical hernia repair with a linea alba plication (through the same incision). This reduces the recurrence risk and improves cosmetic outcome. A preperitoneal mesh can be placed to cover both the hernia defect and the reinforced linea alba.
managementPearls.evidence: Köckerling F et al., Hernia 2020; Diastasis classification studies

operativeNuances.nuance: In the onlay mesh repair for umbilical hernia, after dissecting the subcutaneous flaps 3-4 cm around the defect, identify the sac and reduce it (or excise it if large). Close the fascial defect with interrupted 1-0 slowly absorbable or non-absorbable sutures. Place a 10×10 cm polypropylene mesh over the closed defect, ensuring 3 cm overlap circumferentially. Fix the mesh with interrupted sutures to the anterior rectus sheath at the four quadrants, then continuous suture around the periphery.
operativeNuances.evidence: Köckerling F et al., Hernia 2018; Herniamed technique

operativeNuances.nuance: In the preperitoneal mesh repair for umbilical hernia, the mesh is placed in the preperitoneal space behind the rectus muscles rather than on top of the fascia. This places the mesh in a well-vascularized plane and avoids contact with subcutaneous tissue (lower seroma rate). Access the preperitoneal space by incising the posterior rectus sheath or the linea alba just above the umbilicus and dissecting behind the rectus.
operativeNuances.evidence: Rives-Stoppa technique applied to umbilical hernia; Stoppa RE, World J Surg 1989

operativeNuances.nuance: In the laparoscopic IPOM repair, place the patient in supine position with the surgeon standing on the side opposite the hernia (for a direct view). Insert a 5 mm trocar in the left upper quadrant (Palmer's point) or use an optical trocar. After reducing the hernia contents, measure the defect with a spinal needle under laparoscopic visualization. Place a composite mesh with at least 5 cm overlap, fixed with double-crown tacks and at least 4 transfascial sutures.
operativeNuances.evidence: Heniford BT et al., Ann Surg 2000; Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014

operativeNuances.nuance: For the Mayo repair technique: after excising the hernia sac and reducing the contents, the defect is closed by overlapping the fascial edges. Place interrupted non-absorbable sutures (1-0 or 0) through both edges, suture the lower flap UNDER the upper flap (like a vest-over-pants), then suture the upper flap OVER the lower flap. This creates a double-layer closure. The fascial overlap narrows the umbilical ring significantly — ensure it does not compress any structure.

In emergency repair for an incarcerated umbilical hernia, a midline laparotomy (or extension of the umbilical incision into a midline incision) is often necessary to adequately assess and mobilize the bowel. Do NOT attempt to resect and anastomose bowel through a small umbilical incision — the exposure is inadequate and the anastomosis will be under tension. Extend the incision or perform a separate midline laparotomy.
operativeNuances.evidence: Malek S et al., Hernia 2004; WSES Guidelines 2022

operativeNuances.nuance: After umbilical hernia repair, the cosmetic outcome is important to patients. If the umbilicus is excised (in large hernias or when skin is necrotic), a neoumbilicus can be created: pass a suture from the dermis to the fascia at the midline and cinch it down to recreate the umbilical depression. Alternatively, leave the native umbilicus in place if it is viable and can be preserved.
operativeNuances.evidence: Surgical technique textbooks; cosmetic outcomes studies

operativeNuances.nuance: In the preperitoneal sublay repair for umbilical hernia, the dissection plane is behind the rectus muscles but anterior to the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum. Access this plane by incising the linea alba 2 cm above the umbilicus and entering the retromuscular space. Develop the plane caudally until you are 5 cm below the umbilicus. Reduce the hernia sac from above. Place a 10x15 cm mesh in this preperitoneal pocket, ensuring 5 cm overlap circumferentially. This approach avoids contact between the mesh and the subcutaneous tissue, significantly reducing seroma rates.
operativeNuances.evidence: Rives-Stoppa technique; Chevrel JP, Hernia repair 2000

operativeNuances.nuance: In the laparoscopic IPOM repair for umbilical hernia, the crucial step is the measurement of the defect size. Insert a spinal needle through the abdominal wall at the lateral edge of the defect and measure the distance to the opposite edge under direct laparoscopic vision. The defect is often larger than it appears from the outside because the fascial edges retract laterally. Choose a mesh that provides at least 5 cm overlap. Fix the mesh with double-crown tacks (two concentric rings of tacks at 1-2 cm intervals) and at least 4 transfascial sutures placed at the 12, 3, 6, and 9 o'clock positions.
operativeNuances.evidence: Heniford BT et al., Ann Surg 2000; Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014

operativeNuances.nuance: When repairing an incarcerated umbilical hernia through an open approach, the surgeon must be prepared for a 'second look' into the abdomen through the defect. If the bowel is non-viable, extend the incision into a midline laparotomy from the xiphoid to the pubis. Do NOT attempt to resect bowel through a small umbilical incision — the exposure is inadequate, the anastomosis will be under tension, and the risk of missing additional pathology is high.
operativeNuances.evidence: Malek S et al., Hernia 2004; WSES Guidelines 2022

prognosis.naturalHistory: Infantile umbilical hernia: 90% close spontaneously by age 4-5 years. Those that persist beyond age 5-6 are unlikely to close and require surgical repair. Defects >1.5 cm have a 60% spontaneous closure rate (vs 95% for <1.5 cm). Adult umbilical hernia: progressive enlargement over years. Small defects (<2 cm) paradoxically have higher incarceration/strangulation risk (5-10% per year) than large defects. The risk of incarceration is approximately 3-5% per year for untreated adult umbilical hernias.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After primary suture repair (Mayo or simple): 10-20% at 5 years. After onlay mesh repair: 3-5% at 5 years. After preperitoneal mesh repair: 2-4% at 5 years. After laparoscopic IPOM: 2-5% at 5 years. Recurrence is higher in obese patients, smokers, diabetics, and patients with ascites. Recurrence after mesh repair is often at the mesh edge (inadequate overlap) or due to mesh migration.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Umbilical hernia itself does not increase cancer risk. However, a new-onset umbilical hernia in an adult should prompt investigation for conditions that increase intra-abdominal pressure, including: ovarian cancer (consider pelvic ultrasound in women), colon cancer (consider colonoscopy), and gastric cancer (presenting with ascites). The 'Sister Mary Joseph nodule' is a malignant umbilical nodule from intra-abdominal metastasis — any firm, fixed umbilical mass should be biopsied.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Untreated umbilical hernia causes cosmetic concern, discomfort with activity, and anxiety about the hernia getting bigger or becoming stuck. After successful repair, >90% of adults report significant improvement in quality of life. The main postoperative issues are seroma (10-15%, usually self-limiting), wound infection (2-5%), and recurrence (3-5%). Cosmetic satisfaction is generally high, especially if the umbilicus is preserved or reconstructed.
prognosis.mortality: Elective umbilical hernia repair: mortality <0.1% (one of the safest surgical procedures). Emergency repair for incarceration (viable bowel): 1-3% mortality. Emergency repair for strangulation with bowel resection: 5-10% mortality. Emergency repair in patients with ascites: 5-15% mortality. The mortality is almost entirely due to the underlying comorbidities (liver disease, obesity, cardiovascular disease) rather than the hernia repair itself.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: obesity (BMI >35), ascites (Child-Pugh B/C), age >65 years, diabetes, smoking, immunosuppression, multiple recurrences, and emergency presentation. Protective factors: healthy weight, non-smoker, primary (first-time) repair, elective repair, defect <2 cm, onlay or preperitoneal mesh placement, and absence of ascites.
prognosis.ascitesSpecificPrognosis: In patients with cirrhosis and ascites, the prognosis depends primarily on liver function rather than the hernia itself. Child-Pugh Class A patients can undergo elective repair with morbidity similar to non-cirrhotic patients (wound complications 5-10%). Child-Pugh Class B patients have a 15-25% wound complication rate and 5-10% mortality. Child-Pugh Class C patients should NOT undergo elective repair — manage ascites aggressively and consider TIPS before any surgical intervention. Emergency repair in this group carries 30-50% mortality.
prognosis.collagenMetabolismUmbilical: The molecular profile of the umbilical fascia differs from other hernias: (1) higher baseline elastin content (30% of total matrix vs 10% in the inguinal region), which makes the tissue more compliant but less resilient to repetitive stress, (2) lower total collagen content per unit area, (3) MMP-1 and MMP-8 (collagenases that cleave intact collagen fibrils) are more highly expressed than MMP-2 and MMP-9 (gelatinases that degrade denatured collagen), (4) TIMP-1 appears to be selectively upregulated in patients whose hernias close spontaneously in infancy, suggesting a protective role, and (5) leptin receptors on fascial fibroblasts are upregulated in obesity, and leptin signaling directly stimulates MMP production and inhibits procollagen synthesis through the JAK/STAT pathway.
prognosis.biomechanicalPrinciples: The umbilicus is the site of maximum stress concentration in the anterior abdominal wall during increases in intra-abdominal pressure. Finite element analysis shows that the stress at the umbilicus is 2.5x higher than at the linea alba above or below it, and 4x higher than at the lateral abdominal wall. This is because: (1) the umbilical ring is a circular discontinuity in the midline aponeurosis, and (2) the rectus muscles pull laterally from the ring, creating a 'stress riser' effect. When the umbilical fascia weakens, the defect enlarges preferentially in the transverse direction (following the direction of maximum stress), which explains why umbilical hernias are typically transverse rather than longitudinal defects.

presentingComplaints: A bulge at the belly button that appears when crying (infants) or straining (adults)
The bulge reduces when lying down or with gentle pressure
Discomfort or pain at the umbilicus, especially with bending or lifting
Nausea and vomiting if the hernia becomes stuck
A noticeable increase in size over time

history_questions.question: When did you first notice the belly button bulge — was it present at birth?
Does the bulge appear when crying (infant) or with straining/coughing (adult)?
Can you push the bulge back in? Does it stay in or pop back out?
Do you have any pain or discomfort at the umbilicus?
Have you had any episodes where the bulge got stuck and painful?
Have you had nausea, vomiting, or abdominal swelling?
For infants: was the baby full-term or premature?
Has the hernia been getting bigger as the child grows?
How many children have you had (for adult women)?
Do you have a chronic cough, constipation, or difficulty urinating?
Are you overweight or obese?
Do you have any swelling in your legs or abdomen (suggesting ascites)?
Have you ever been diagnosed with liver disease or kidney failure?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Do you have a family history of umbilical hernias?
Have you had any previous hernia repairs that failed?
Do you smoke?
For adults: has the skin over the hernia ever become red, thin, or broken?
Do you do heavy lifting at work (farming, construction)?
Have you tried any home remedies, binders, or coins to hold the hernia in?
Have you had any abdominal surgeries that involved the umbilicus (e.g., laparoscopy, gallbladder surgery)?
Do you have any liver disease or history of jaundice, hepatitis, or alcohol use?
Are you currently on any diuretics or medications for ascites?
For women: have you noticed any irregular periods, bloating, or pelvic symptoms that could suggest ovarian pathology?
Have you had any CT scan or ultrasound of the hernia? What did it show?
Do you have any difficulty with breathing or shortness of breath (suggesting large hernia with loss of domain or ascites)?
Have you noticed any 'flooding' — fluid leaking from the belly button?
Do you have any history of pancreatitis, chronic liver disease, or hepatitis?
Have you ever had a diagnosis of cirrhosis or portal hypertension?
Do you consume alcohol regularly? How much and for how long?
Have you had any jaundice (yellowing of the eyes or skin)?
Do you have any swelling in your legs or feet (suggesting fluid overload or liver disease)?
Have you been diagnosed with heart failure or kidney failure?
Have you had a liver biopsy or been told you have fatty liver disease?
Do you have any chronic infection like hepatitis B, hepatitis C, or schistosomiasis?
Have you ever had a paracentesis (drainage of abdominal fluid)?
Are you on any diuretic medication (water tablets) for any reason?
Has the hernia ever leaked fluid that soaked through your clothes?
Do you have a family history of umbilical hernia, especially in siblings or parents?

examination_findings.finding: Palpable mass at the umbilicus that increases with crying/straining
Positive cough impulse at the umbilical ring
Palpable fascial defect at the umbilicus — measure the diameter
Reducibility assessment — can the mass be reduced into the abdomen
Irreducible and tender mass — indicates incarceration
Overlying skin thinning, ulceration, or erythema
Signs of bowel obstruction if incarcerated with bowel involvement
Fever and tachycardia suggesting strangulation
In infants: defect <1cm and reducible — majority close spontaneously
In adults: assess for ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill)
Multiple defects (Swiss cheese) — uncommon at umbilicus
Rectus diastasis may accompany umbilical hernia
Bowel sounds auscultable over the hernia mass
In patients with ascites, a fluid thrill may be palpable through the hernia sac
Skin over the hernia may have an 'orange peel' appearance (peau d'orange) from chronic edema
In thin patients with small hernias, the fascial defect is felt as a sharp, well-defined ring at the umbilicus
The hernia may be tender to deep palpation even when reducible, suggesting omental adhesions within the sac
In patients with ascites: inspect for shifting dullness, fluid thrill, and a fluid wave across the abdomen — the hernia sac may be filled with ascitic fluid rather than bowel or omentum
Caput medusae (dilated periumbilical veins) indicates portal hypertension and should be distinguished from the hernia mass itself
The 'fluid thrill' sign: tap one side of the hernia sac and feel the transmitted impulse on the opposite side — positive when the sac contains fluid (ascites, seroma) rather than solid contents
Skin over the hernia may be excoriated, macerated, or have a 'weeping' appearance from chronic moisture or ascitic fluid leakage
In patients with small defects (<2 cm), the hernia may only be palpable with the tip of the examining finger inserted into the umbilicus, feeling the fascial edge as a sharp, well-defined ring
The hernia may be more prominent when the patient sits up from a supine position (increases intra-abdominal pressure) compared to standing — this is the 'sit-up test'
In large hernias with loss of domain, the hernia contents may contain colon (tympanic percussion note) and omentum (dull percussion note) simultaneously
The 'cough impulse' may be paradoxically absent in large umbilical hernias (>5 cm) because the wide defect does not create a pressure gradient to transmit the impulse — do not be falsely reassured

bedside_scores.name: Umbilical Hernia Repair Priority Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Infants: 0-4 observe, 5+ consider early repair. Adults: 0-3 elective repair, 4+ urgent repair during same admission

riskFactors.note: Most important modifiable risk factor in adults

subtypes.name: Infantile Umbilical Hernia
subtypes.management: Observe until age 4-5 years. Majority (90%) close spontaneously by age 4. Surgical repair if: persists beyond age 5 years, enlarging, becomes symptomatic, or incarcerated. Most surgeons now recommend repair of defects >1.5cm by age 2-3. Simple primary closure (Mayo or simple fascial closure). General anaesthesia, day case.

subtypes.name: Adult Umbilical Hernia
subtypes.management: Elective repair recommended for all adult umbilical hernias due to incarceration risk. Small defects (<2cm): primary sutured repair (Mayo or simple closure) — acceptable but higher recurrence than mesh. Mesh repair preferred for all: onlay mesh (suprafascial) or sublay (retromuscular/preperitoneal). Laparoscopic IPOM for larger defects. Emergency repair if incarcerated/strangulated. Address predisposing factors (weight loss, ascites management).

subtypes.name: Recurrent Umbilical Hernia
subtypes.management: CT evaluation to map the defect relative to the previous mesh. If previous suture repair: standard mesh repair (onlay or preperitoneal). If previous mesh repair: options include (a) laparoscopic IPOM over the previous mesh, (b) preperitoneal mesh through virgin plane, or (c) component separation for massive recurrent defects. Mesh infection must be excluded before re-do repair.

bedside.name: Reducibility assessment
bedside.indication: determine if hernia is reducible, incarcerated, or strangulated

laboratory.name: Liver function tests / Albumin
laboratory.indication: if ascites suspected from liver disease

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: confirm diagnosis, assess contents (omentum vs bowel), defect size, Doppler for vascularity

imaging.name: CT Abdomen
imaging.indication: if complicated, obese, suspected ascites, or planning large/complex repair

interpretation_rules.spontaneous_closure_in_infants: >90% close by age 4-5 years. Those that persist beyond age 5-6 need repair. Larger defects (>1.5cm) are less likely to close spontaneously — some advocate earlier repair.
interpretation_rules.adult_mesh_repair: Mesh repair reduces recurrence rate from 10-20% (primary suture) to <5%. Current evidence supports mesh repair for all adult umbilical hernias, even small defects.
interpretation_rules.ascites_association: New onset umbilical hernia in an adult with ascites requires urgent assessment. The hernia can rapidly enlarge, cause skin breakdown, and leak ascitic fluid ('flooding'). Manage ascites first (diuretics, paracentesis), then repair.
interpretation_rules.small_defect_risk: Small umbilical defects (1-2cm) in adults have a HIGHER strangulation risk than large defects because the narrow ring compresses contents more tightly. Do not dismiss small hernias.

management.protocolReference: International Endohernia Society (IEHS) Guidelines for Umbilical Hernia (2022)
management.initial: Infant: reassure parents, explain natural history of spontaneous closure. Adult: Assess for underlying causes (ascites, obesity, chronic cough). Weight loss counseling. Control comorbidities. CT if large/complex. Elective repair recommended for all adults.
management.definitive: Infants (persistent >age 4-5): primary fascial closure (simple closure or Mayo repair). Suture only — no mesh. Day case. Adults: Small (<2cm): Can be primary sutured repair or onlay mesh. Larger (≥2cm): Mesh repair recommended. Onlay mesh (preperitoneal or suprafascial). Sublay mesh (retromuscular). Laparoscopic IPOM for larger defects. Emergency (incarcerated/strangulated): Open repair. Assess bowel viability. Resect if non-viable. Mesh repair if clean; primary repair + delayed mesh if contaminated.
management.postop: Abdominal binder for comfort (2-4 weeks). Activity restriction: no heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks. Wound care. Manage seroma (common with onlay mesh). Address predisposing factors long-term.

pathways.treatment: Observation. Spontaneous closure expected by age 4-5. Reassure parents. If persists beyond 5 years or enlarges: elective repair.

pathways.criteria: reducible, symptomatic or enlarging, defect any size
pathways.treatment: Elective repair. Small defect (<2cm): primary suture or onlay mesh. Larger defect (>=2cm): mesh repair (onlay, sublay, or laparoscopic IPOM).
Emergency repair. Open approach. Assess bowel viability. Resection if non-viable. Mesh repair if no contamination. Primary repair + interval mesh if contaminated. Manage sepsis.

operative_templates.procedure: Umbilical Hernia Repair — Mayo (Primary Tissue Repair)
operative_templates.indication: Small umbilical hernia defect <2cm in adults; primary repair in infants
operative_templates.incision: Infra-umbilical curvilinear incision or vertical through umbilicus
steps: Curvilinear incision at the infra-umbilical crease
Dissect the hernia sac from the subcutaneous tissue
Open the sac, reduce contents (omentum or bowel)
For Mayo repair: overlap the fascial edges in a vest-over-pants fashion with non-absorbable sutures
For simple repair: close the fascial defect transversely with interrupted non-absorbable sutures
Reattach the umbilicus to the fascia (if desired for cosmesis)

operative_templates.procedure: Umbilical Hernia Repair with Onlay Mesh
operative_templates.incision: Vertical incision through the umbilicus or curvilinear infra-umbilical
steps: Skin incision, dissect subcutaneous flaps 3-4cm around the defect
Identify the hernia sac and fascial defect
Open the sac, reduce contents, excise the sac
Close the fascial defect with interrupted non-absorbable sutures (loosely or after mesh placement)
Place a polypropylene mesh (size such that it overlaps the defect by 3cm in all directions)
Fix the mesh to the anterior rectus fascia circumferentially with interrupted sutures
If preperitoneal approach: dissect a pocket in the preperitoneal space and place mesh there
Place a drain if extensive dissection
Recreate the umbilical depression (optional) with a suture from the dermis to the fascia

patient_education.explanation: An umbilical hernia is a protrusion through the belly button. In infants, it is usually a normal condition that heals on its own by age 4-5. In adults, it is caused by weakness of the abdominal wall from obesity, multiple pregnancies, ascites (fluid in the abdomen), or chronic straining. It will not heal on its own in adults and requires surgery. Small hernias can be dangerous because the tight opening can trap the bowel and cut off its blood supply.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if: the hernia becomes hard, painful, and cannot be pushed back, you develop nausea or vomiting, your abdomen becomes swollen, the skin over the hernia becomes red or black, or you have fever with chills.
patient_education.activity: In infants: no activity restrictions. In adults: no heavy lifting (more than 5kg) for 4 weeks after mesh repair. Walking is encouraged. Return to desk work in 1-2 weeks, manual labour or farm work in 4-6 weeks. Use an abdominal binder for comfort if provided.
patient_education.diet: Eat a diet that prevents constipation: fruits, vegetables, whole grains. Drink 6-8 glasses of water daily. Use stool softeners if needed. Weight loss is strongly recommended if you are overweight.
patient_education.medication: Take pain medication as prescribed. Keep the wound clean and dry. Infants: no specific care needed — the hernia will be observed. If you have ascites from liver disease, you must continue your diuretic medications and follow up with your medical doctor.
patient_education.followUp: Infants: return at age 4-5 to reassess closure. Adults: return in 1-2 weeks for wound check. If you had an incarcerated hernia repaired, you may need longer follow-up. Weight loss and lifestyle modification are key to preventing recurrence.

followup.before_discharge: Wound check. Activity restriction counseling. Abdominal binder if provided. Infant: educate parents on observation and warning signs.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check, assess for seroma, infection. Review activity restrictions.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Wound review and suture removal. Discuss return to activity.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Check for recurrence. Full activity clearance. Weight loss counseling.
after_discharge.3_months: Only if ongoing concerns. Long-term recurrence surveillance.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe pain, fever, wound discharge or redness, recurrence of bulge with tenderness, or any incarceration signs.
